turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in Case 22-340, Pulsifer versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Congress sometimes establishes mandatory minimum penalties for crimes, but federal sentencing law also contains what’s called a Safety Valve Provision, which exempts some defendants from the mandatory minimums. So a Judge can, if she wants, impose a lighter sentence. To qualify for safety valve relief, a defendant has to meet five requirements. One of those in Paragraph (f)(1), concerns the defendant’s criminal history, and that’s the requirement this case is about. Mark Pulsifer was convicted of distributing a large amount of methamphetamine. That crime would require a 15-year mandatory minimum, unless Pulsifer is eligible for the safety valve. He met all the other requirements. The only question was whether his criminal history made him ineligible. Pulsifer had two prior convictions for what the sentencing guidelines call three-point offenses. That means offenses for which Pulsifer served at least 13 months in prison. So, for Pulsifer, the question here is, if a person has two prior three-point offenses, is he eligible for safety valve relief. Now, you might not think so, but that ends up being quite an interesting question about interpreting statutes, because there are two possible grammatical ways of reading this statute, one favoring Pulsifer and the other not. Here’s how the statute works. It says, a person is eligible for safety valve relief if he does not have three things, and then it gives a list, A, B, and C. Subparagraph A is more than four total criminal history points. Subparagraph B is a three-point offense, the kind I just told you about, and subparagraph C is a violent two-point offense. That means an offense for which a person got less than 13 months in prison, but which is of a violent nature. Pulsifer says he meets that test because he doesn’t have one of those three things. It’s true he has more than four criminal history points because he has two, three-point offenses, three plus three equals six, more than four. It’s true he has a three-point offense. Actually, he has two of them, but he happens not to have a two-point offense, and he says that makes all the difference. As long as he doesn’t have the combination of those three things, the full package, he’s entitled to relief. The government says no. It reads the statute as a checklist. A person is entitled to relief only if he doesn’t have four criminal history points, and he doesn’t have a three-point offense, and he doesn’t have a two-point violent offense. And from that perspective, Pulsifer fails the test because he has two of those three things. It doesn’t matter that he happens not to have the third. He can’t get through the checklist. Again, the opinion starts by saying that as a matter of pure grammar, if you don’t look at the content and context of the provision, if you just look at it really abstractly, either one of those two meanings of the phrase does not have A, B, and C is possible. But when we interpret statutes, we do look at content and context, and when we do that, we think it pretty clear that Pulsifer loses, because reading the statute Pulsifer’s way would create two big problems. First, Pulsifer’s reading fails to separate defendants with more serious criminal histories from defendants with less serious histories. Imagine a person with 10 three-point offenses who happens not to have a two-point offense. He only commits very serious crimes. The statute is designed to exclude that person from safety valve relief, and that’s in keeping with the whole thrust of the sentencing guidelines. But under Pulsifer’s view, he could get safety valve relief even while a person with a much less serious criminal record, say a person with one three-point and one two-point offense could not. Second, Pulsifer’s reading makes a big chunk of the provision, makes subparagraph A utterly superfluous. Subparagraph A is the one that says a defendant can’t have more than four criminal history points. Under Pulsifer’s view, that subparagraph has absolutely no work to do. That’s because if a defendant has a three-point offense under subparagraph B and a two-point offense under subparagraph C, he’s always going to have more than four criminal history points. Three plus two is five, more than four. The government’s reading doesn’t have that problem because under its reading, each subparagraph operates independently, not as part of a single package. So the long and short of it is that content and context reduce two possible readings to one. Paragraph (f)(1) is a checklist. A person is eligible for relief only if he doesn’t have more than four criminal history points, doesn’t have a three-point offense, and doesn’t have a violent two-point offense. Because Pulsifer can’t get through that checklist, he can’t escape a mandatory minimum. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Sotomayor and Justice Jackson joined.
